meta.title: mort

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Split from the source PDF’s "mort, necro" entry: mort is Latin and necro is Greek, and a plant’s root.origin_language can only hold one value, so an honest split was needed. necro is its sibling plant.

root.label: mort
root.mentor_note: mort is Latin for death; a separate Greek root, necro, means the same thing from a different direction.

attempt.direction_prompt: Immune to death, or subject to it like everything else?

context_sentences: For all his talk of legacy, the general remained as mortal as any soldier under his command.
The play’s central question is a simple one: what does a mortal owe to a god?
The hospital’s mortuary was two floors below the maternity ward, a contrast nobody mentioned aloud.
A clerical error in the mortuary’s records delayed the funeral by nearly a week.
His mortification was total once he realised he had sent the email to the entire company.
She still remembers the mortification of tripping on stage in front of the whole school.

construct_options.text: A feeling of intense shame or embarrassment
The physical process of a body decaying after death
A feeling of pride following a great achievement

context_sentences: The myth’s hero begs the gods for a single day as an immortal, and instantly regrets it.
No line in the play is more quoted, more immortal, than its closing couplet.

construct_options.text: Living forever, not subject to death
A feeling of deep shame or embarrassment
A room where the dead are kept before burial
